title: Trump’s Iran Deal: What We Know, What's Contested, and What Remains Unresolved | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: The US and Iran reached a June 14 memorandum of understanding to end a 3.5-month war that closed the Strait of Hormuz and roiled oil markets; CFR analysts break down the six contested issues (Hormuz reopening, nuclear program, proxies, missiles, sanctions/compensation, Lebanon) and warn the deal is fragile, with oil normalization taking months.

insights.claim: The US-Iran MOU starts by reopening the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint for nearly one-fifth of the world's oil and natural gas supply, with the US removing its naval blockade and Iran reopening the waterway to commercial shipping.
insights.speaker: Phil McCausland
insights.so_what: Hormuz reopening is the single biggest near-term oil-supply catalyst; the sequencing and verification of the blockade lift will drive crude price direction.
insights.quote: The initial pact—a memorandum of understanding (MOU)—will start with reopening the Strait of Hormuz, a choke point for nearly one-fifth of the world’s oil and natural gas supply

insights.claim: Oil dropped roughly $4 per barrel on the deal announcement even before the strait officially reopened, but experts say it will likely take several months for oil flow to normalize, with minesweeping of the waterbed alone taking weeks.
insights.speaker: Phil McCausland
insights.so_what: The gap between headline relief and physical normalization means crude could stay elevated for months; fade the initial price drop if positioned on supply tightness.
insights.quote: But experts indicated that it would still likely take several months for the flow of oil to return to normal after the Strait of Hormuz is reopened. Scouring the waterbed for mines alone will likely take weeks.
insights.locator: The price of oil dropped to its lowest point

insights.claim: Iran may impose service fees or 'environment fees' on Hormuz transits despite agreeing not to charge formal 'tolls,' and reopening will reportedly be subject to unspecified 'Iranian arrangements.'
insights.speaker: Elisa Ewers
insights.so_what: Hidden transit costs would raise structural shipping and energy costs and could set precedent for other chokepoints; watch for fee mechanisms in the final terms.
insights.quote: While Iran may agree to not charge ‘tolls,’ service fees and other mechanisms have been floated
insights.locator: How the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz is managed

insights.claim: The US is reportedly pushing for a twenty-year suspension of Iran's enrichment program while Iran won't go above ten years, leaving the pause duration unresolved for the sixty-day negotiation window.
insights.speaker: Phil McCausland
insights.so_what: The duration gap is a concrete breakdown risk; a failure here could reignite conflict and re-spike oil, making the 60-day window a key event to monitor.
insights.quote: The United States is reportedly pushing for twenty years, while Iran reportedly won’t go above ten

insights.claim: Iran's commitment to never pursue a nuclear weapon is one it 'made and crossed before,' and it has not made a definitive public commitment, postponing final negotiations until the other party implements its memorandum obligations.
insights.speaker: Phil McCausland
insights.so_what: Past noncompliance plus a conditional public stance signals elevated deal-collapse risk; discount optimistic peace pricing.
insights.quote: Iran would, however, commit to never pursuing a nuclear weapon—a commitment it made and crossed before.

insights.claim: Verification is critical because Iran can hide enrichment by building many small workshops of advanced centrifuges, challenging inspectors to find them all.
insights.speaker: Ray Takeyh
insights.so_what: Weak verification leaves a latent breakout path, keeping a geopolitical risk premium embedded in Middle East energy assets.
insights.quote: By building many small workshops containing advanced centrifuges, Tehran can challenge the prying foreigners to find them all

insights.claim: US intelligence estimates Iran retains roughly 70 percent of its prewar missile stockpile and 70 percent of its mobile missile launchers, and may be using the ceasefire to rebuild its military base faster than expected.
insights.speaker: Phil McCausland
insights.so_what: A largely intact Iranian missile force means residual escalation capability survives any deal; regional risk premium should not fully deflate.
insights.quote: U.S. intelligence sources estimate that Iran retains 70 percent of its prewar missile stockpile and roughly 70 percent of its mobile missile launchers.

insights.claim: Iran has cast its ballistic missile program as a non-negotiable red line and its counterproposal omits missiles entirely, while the US insists any deal address missile capabilities—analysts believe this demand has quietly diminished.
insights.speaker: Phil McCausland
insights.so_what: If the US drops the missile demand to close the deal, it signals prioritizing oil-flow restoration over long-term containment.
insights.quote: Iran has cast changes to its missile program as an intractable red line

insights.claim: Iran claims $270 billion in war damages and demands immediate access to ~$24 billion in frozen assets, while Washington wants assets to stay frozen until a verifiably implemented deal—neither side has shown flexibility on timing.
insights.speaker: Phil McCausland
insights.so_what: The sanctions-relief sequencing fight is a deal fulcrum; staged asset release would gate Iran's reentry into the world economy and oil markets.
insights.quote: Iran has reportedly asserted that it should be able to immediately access about $24 billion that the United States and its allies have frozen

insights.claim: Iran's economy is running at nearly 70 percent annual inflation and faced widespread protests earlier in the year, creating strong domestic pressure to secure sanctions relief.
insights.speaker: Phil McCausland
insights.so_what: Acute economic distress raises Iran's incentive to deal, a bullish factor for eventual oil normalization—but also for desperate hardball.
insights.quote: stabilize a wartime economy running at nearly 70 percent of annual inflation

insights.claim: Israel is not party to the US-Iran MOU and Israeli officials appear unhappy with it; Israel struck Beirut on June 14 killing three even as the deal broke, with ministers stating 'Trump's agreement does not bind us.'
insights.speaker: Phil McCausland
insights.so_what: Israel as an unbound spoiler is the clearest path to deal collapse and renewed oil-supply disruption; monitor the Israel-Lebanon front as the live escalation vector.
insights.quote: Trump’s agreement does not bind us... we are not party to this agreement. It does not safeguard our security.

insights.claim: Iran has explicitly linked ending Israel's war in Lebanon to any Iran-US ceasefire, calling it an 'inseparable part' of the agreement, and briefly suspended negotiations over Israeli operations there.
insights.speaker: Phil McCausland
insights.so_what: Lebanon is the binding constraint on the whole deal; continued Israeli strikes can unravel Iran's commitments and the Hormuz reopening.
insights.quote: While Iran has said that ending the war in Lebanon is an “inseparable part” of the agreement to end the Iran war, Israel has framed its Lebanon campaign as a separate and ongoing objective

insights.claim: CFR's Steven Cook warns the parties have reached deals before only to find they cannot bridge remaining gaps, and negotiations on the nuclear program will be long and difficult.
insights.speaker: Steven Cook
insights.so_what: Tempers the market's relief rally; the MOU is a starting point, not a settlement, so expect volatility through the 60-day window.
insights.quote: We have been here before only to discover the parties cannot bridge the remaining gaps
